steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage has two parts: the work’s strengths (comprehensive, unique, valuable resource) and one clear limitation (lacks an authorial point of view to unify the accounts). The main idea must capture both without adding unsupported claims.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — *The Fifty-Year Mission* includes more accounts from people involved with *Star Trek* television shows than it does from people involved with *Star Trek* films.: the passage says the book includes cast and crew from every series and film; it never compares TV vs. film coverage. ✗
  • Choice B — The compilers of *The Fifty-Year Mission* had lofty goals for their oral history of the *Star Trek* franchise, but the published work lacks information about many key events in the franchise's history.: the passage never says information about events is missing — the limitation is the lack of authorial perspective, not gaps in coverage. ✗
  • Choice C — The large amount of material compiled into *The Fifty-Year Mission* is surprising given that many of the people involved in the *Star Trek* franchise did not participate in the oral history project.: the passage makes no mention of non-participants; this is invented. ✗
  • Choice D — *The Fifty-Year Mission* represents a worthwhile attempt to thoroughly recount the history of the *Star Trek* franchise, but its approach has an important limitation.: directly reflects both halves of the passage — the work’s value as a comprehensive resource and the shortcoming of lacking a unifying authorial voice. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
